coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2013 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zong, Jie
Yang, Xiaoling
Trinchi, Adrian
Hardin, Simon
Cole, Ivan
Zhu, Yihua
Li, Chunzhong
Muster, Tim
Wei, Gang
coredata.dc:description: Copper ion (Cu2+) and l-cysteine (l-Cys) detection is critically important since an abnormal level of Cu2+ or l-Cys is an indicator for many diseases. In this paper, we demonstrate an “off–on” approach for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes. CDs were prepared by using mesoporous silica (MS) spheres as nanoreactors. The binding ability of CDs towards metal ions was examined by comparing the fluorescence intensities of CDs before and after the addition of the metal ions. The addition of Cu2+ cations leads to their absorption on the surface of CDs and the significant fluorescence quench of CDs (turn-off). The resulting in CDs-Cu2+ system was found to be sensitive to l-Cys. The addition of l-Cys not only serves to shelter the CDs effectively from being quenched, but also to reverse the quenching and restore the fluorescence (turn-on) due to its ability to remove Cu2+ from the surface of CDs. This method is facile, rapid, low cost, and environment-friendly. A detection limit as low as 2.3×10−8 M for Cu2+ and 3.4×10−10 M for l-Cys is obtained, which is promising for biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mesoporous silica
Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Copper ions
l-cysteine
“off–on” detection.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ and l-Cys
Mechanism of the “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) CDs/MS composite spheres and (b) CDs (inset: the higher magnification image of CDs). (c) UV–vis absorption and PL emission spectra for the CDs dispersed in water. The emission spectrum was obtained under excitation at 320nm. (d) FT-IR spectrum of the as-prepared CDs.

(a) Fluorescence response of CDs in the presence of increasing concentration of Cu2+ (from a to m): 0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, 1, 2, 4, 8, 10, 20, 40, 80, and 100μM. (b) Fluorescence intensity changes of the CDs in the presence of increasing concentration of Cu2+. (Inset: a linear relationship of (F1/F0)−1 versus the concentration of Cu2+ over the range from 0 to 10μM.) λ ex=320nm. The error bar represents the standard deviation of three measurements.
Selectivity of Cu2+ using CDs as probes. The concentration of all the metal ions is 10μM. λ ex=320nm. The error bar represents the standard deviation of three measurements.

(a) Fluorescence response of CDs-Cu2+ in the presence of increasing concentration of l-Cys (from a to e): 0, 4, 8, 10, and 30μM. (b) A linear relationship of F2/F1 versus the concentration of l-Cys over the range from 0 to 30μM. λ ex=320nm. The error bar represents the standard deviation of three measurements.
Fluorescence response of CDs-Cu2+ in the absence and presence of amino acid or GSH with a concentration of 30μM. λ ex=320nm.
Schematic illustration of Cu2+ and l-Cys detection using CDs.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Ultrafine Materials of Ministry of Education, School of Materials Science and Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIRO Materials Science and Engineering (CMSE), Clayton, VIC 3168, Australia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIRO Materials Science and Engineering (CMSE), Lindfield, NSW 2070, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper ion (Cu2+) and l-cysteine (l-Cys) detection is critically important since an abnormal level of Cu2+ or l-Cys is an indicator for many diseases. In this paper, we demonstrate an “off–on” approach for highly sensitive and selective detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys using carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent probes. CDs were prepared by using mesoporous silica (MS) spheres as nanoreactors. The binding ability of CDs towards metal ions was examined by comparing the fluorescence intensities of CDs before and after the addition of the metal ions. The addition of Cu2+ cations leads to their absorption on the surface of CDs and the significant fluorescence quench of CDs (turn-off). The resulting in CDs-Cu2+ system was found to be sensitive to l-Cys. The addition of l-Cys not only serves to shelter the CDs effectively from being quenched, but also to reverse the quenching and restore the fluorescence (turn-on) due to its ability to remove Cu2+ from the surface of CDs. This method is facile, rapid, low cost, and environment-friendly. A detection limit as low as 2.3×10−8 M for Cu2+ and 3.4×10−10 M for l-Cys is obtained, which is promising for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:para: The study of biologically important cations and thiol-containing amino acids has drawn great attention in recent years due to their crucial roles in a wide range of biological processes (Zhang et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2009; Shao et al., 2005; Kim et al., 2006; Zhou et al., 2012b; Huang et al., 2010; Sudeep et al., 2005). Particularly, emphasis has been given to copper ions (Cu2+), which are the third most abundant transition metal ions after iron and zinc in the human body. Cu2+ is essential in electron transfer processes of many biological reactions (Miao et al., 2012; Yu et al., 2008). However, the deficiency of Cu2+ in the body can affect enzyme activity and inhibit cell metabolism, leading to hematological manifestations and a wide variety of neurological problems, while excess Cu2+ in the body is related to gastrointestinal disturbance and damage to liver and kidneys (Kumar et al., 2012; Liu and Lu, 2007a; Li et al., 2013a; Jung et al., 2009). On the other hand, l-cysteine (l-Cys), as a small-molecular-weight biological thiol-containing amino acid, is essential in maintaining biological redox homeostasis (Leung et al., 2013; Niu et al., 2012; Das et al., 2012). An abnormal level of l-Cys has been linked to many diseases, such as slow growth, liver damage, skin lesions, Alzheimer's disease and cardiovascular disease (Wang et al., 2009; Chen et al., 2012; Li et al., 2012a). In view of the significance of Cu2+ and l-Cys, the sensitive and selective detection of them is very important (Zhou et al., 2012c; Gattás-Asfura et al., 2003; Lee et al., 2008; Jung et al., 2012).

Many methods for the detection Cu2+ and l-Cys have been developed and utilized, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Ghaedi et al., 2007; Matoušek et al., 2008), inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) (Tao and Sturgeon, 1999; Feng et al., 1998), capillary electrophoresis (Li et al., 2007; Inoue and Kirchhoff, 2002), voltammetry (Rahman et al., 2003; Fei et al., 2005), and so on (Huang et al., 2012). Unfortunately, these methods require complicated operation procedures, expensive instrumentation, and large sample volumes (Miao et al., 2012). Recently, highly sensitive and environment-friendly fluorimetric methods for their detection based on DNAzyme have been developed (Miao et al., 2012; Liu and Lu, 2007b). However, the high cost for preparing DNAzyme labeled with electrochemically-active molecules or fluorophores/quenchers has been a limiting factor. In addition, DNAzyme can degrade easily, resulting in unstable DNAzyme-based systems (Li et al., 2013b). Therefore, developing a low-cost, environment-friendly and stable detection method for Cu2+ and l-Cys is highly desirable.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of fluorescent nanoparticles, have attracted increasing attention for their sensing capability due to their chemical inertness and stability, high photostability against photobleaching and blinking, tunable excitation and emission spectra, and excellent biocompatibility (Wang et al., 2010; Bao et al., 2011; Wu et al., 2012). In view of the change of fluorescence intensity of CDs in the detection process, they have been used to monitor many materials, such as thrombin (Wang et al., 2011), glucose (Tang et al., 2012), Hg2+ (Li et al., 2011), and Cu2+ (Liu et al., 2012). Although CDs are low cost, the detection process can be complicated. For example, Wang et al. developed an aptamer biosensor for thrombin based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) from upconverting phosphors (UCPs) to CDs. In this complex multi-step approach, the poly(acrylic acid) functionalized UCPs are tagged with thrombin aptamer, then bound to the surface of CDs (Wang et al., 2011). It is highly desirable to develop a simple detection process. Moreover, l-Cys has been utilized for the chemical accumulation of metal ions (Fan et al., 2012).

Herein, we report “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys by using unmodified CDs as fluorescent probes. This method is facile, low cost, stable, and environmental-friendly. The detection of Cu2+ is based on its quenching effect on the fluorescence of CDs, while the detection of l-Cys is due to the binding preference of l-Cys toward Cu2+ by forming a Cu2+–S bond. The competitive interaction between Cu2+, l-Cys, and CDs removes Cu2+ from the surface of CDs, and thus the fluorescence of the system could be turned on. The fluorescence quenching by Cu2+ and the subsequent fluorescence recovery by l-Cys makes it possible to detect Cu2+ and l-Cys.

Hexadecylamine (90%), reduced glutathione (GSH), and dialysis membranes (regenerated cellulose, molecular weight cut off (MWCO: 1000)) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Co. Citric acid (99.5%) and all other chemicals were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co. All the chemicals were used as received without any further purification. Double distilled water was used in all experiments.

CDs/MS spheres were synthesized as previously described in the literature (Zong et al., 2011) with a little modification. Briefly, 1g of hexadecylamine was dissolved in a mixed solution including 100mL isopropanol and 90mL double distilled water to form a clear solution. Then 1.4mL ammonia (25% w/w) was added, which was followed by the addition of 5.8mL of tetraethoxysilane (TEOS). The mixture was homogenized and kept at room temperature overnight. The resulting white precipitate was collected by filtration of the reaction mixture, washed with ethanol three times and then twice with distilled water, and dried at ambient temperature. Hexadecylamine was removed by calcination at 600°C for 6h in flowing air. Subsequently, 1g of the resulting powder was immersed in 3.7mL of a mixed solution with three ingredients including NaCl, LiCl, and KNO3 (the mass ratio of NaCl, LiCl, KNO3, and H2O is 20:5:5:70). The mixture was dispersed with ultrasound and then calcined at 450°C for 2h in nitrogen atmosphere. MS spheres were obtained. Then, 0.4g of MS spheres and 0.05g of citric acid were soaked in 1mL of mixed solution with three ingredients including NaCl, LiCl, KNO3 and H2O (mass ratio 20:5:5:70). The mixture was dispersed with ultrasonic vibrations and then calcined at 300°C for 2h in air. The resulting powders were denoted as CDs/MS.

To release the CDs, the MS spheres were removed by etching with 2M NaOH solution at 40°C for 24h. The excess NaOH was neutralized by HCl. The resulting mixture was cooled to room temperature and the supernatant retained after centrifugation at 8000rpm. The obtained solution was dialyzed against double distilled water through a dialysis membrane to remove ionic impurities. The homogeneous yellow solution containing CDs were finally obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cu2+ and l-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: 3.00mL of CDs (0.015mg/mL) was placed in a quartz cell of 10mm path length. Then, a specific volume of CuCl2 (10−3 M) was added to the cell and incubated for 15min at room temperature before the spectral measurements. The final concentration of Cu2+ was ranging from 0 to 50μM. For comparison, different metal ions with the final concentration of 10μM were added to 3.00mL of CDs (0.015mg/mL), and incubated for 15min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 320nm.

For the detection of l-Cys, Cu2+ with a final concentration of 10μM was added to 3.00mL of CDs (0.015mg/mL) to form a mixture solution, which was denoted as CDs-Cu2+. Then, different amounts of l-Cys (10−3 M) were added to CDs-Cu2+. The final concentration of l-Cys ranged from 0 to 30μM. After 1min, the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 320nm.

High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were taken on a JEOL JEM 2011 microscope at an acceleration voltage of 200kV. The specimen was prepared by drop casting the sample dispersion onto a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying under room temperature. Photoluminescence spectra were measured on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer. UV–vis spectra of the CDs dispersed in water were obtained using a Unico UV-2012PC spectrophotometer. All spectra were recorded with quartz cells of 10mm path length. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 5700 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer within the range of 500–4000cm−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 shows the mechanism of the “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys using CDs as fluorescent probes. On the one hand, the surface states of CDs can be influenced by metal ions (Zhou et al., 2012a), which exhibits a strong effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs. Due to this effect, CDs are used to detect Cu2+. Initially, when CDs were free in aqueous solution, they showed strong fluorescence intensity. However, in the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence of CDs was quenched significantly through the charge transfer process, which suggests that Cu2+ binds to CDs and the surface state of CDs changes. One key feature of l-Cys lies in its thiol group, which makes l-Cys have the strong binding preference toward Cu2+ by forming Cu2+–S bond. Upon the addition of l-Cys, the competitive interaction between Cu2+, l-Cys, and CDs disturbs the interaction between Cu2+ and CDs. Cu2+ was then able to be removed from the surface of CDs, and thus the fluorescence of the system could be recovered. The different fluorescent responses upon the addition of Cu2+ and l-Cys make CDs possible to be used for Cu2+ and l-Cys detection.

HRTEM images of the CDs/MS composites and CDs derived from CDs/MS composites etched with NaOH are shown in Fig. 1a and b, respectively. It shows that the CDs/MS composites have spherical morphology with a diameter of around 1μm, indicating that the CDs were exclusively synthesized in the pores of MS spheres (Fig. 1a). The CDs obtained by etching the CDs/MS composites with NaOH are near spherical. The size of the CDs is narrowly distributed with diameters in the range of 3.0–4.0nm (Fig. 1b).

The optical properties of the as-prepared CDs dispersed in water are shown in Fig. 1c. Like most luminescent CDs (Wang et al., 2010; Bao et al., 2011), no obvious absorption band can be observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared CDs, while the PL peak appears around 460nm upon excitation at 320nm, with a Stokes shift of 140nm. Using rhodamine B as a standard, the PL quantum yield of CDs was found to be ∼7%. Although the intrinsic mechanism of CD luminescence is still an open question, more and more evidence indicates the fluorescence of CDs can be affected considerably by their surface states (Dong et al., 2012; Bao et al., 2011).

FT-IR spectrum was acquired to determine the surface state of CDs, as shown in Fig. 1d. The peaks at 2856 and 2923cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–H bonds, while the peaks at 579, 626, 1344, and 1465cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibrations of C–H bonds. The peaks at 1090 and 1217cm−1 correspond to the C–O stretching vibrations. The stretching vibrations of O–H (3064 and 3352cm−1) and CåO bonds (1633 and 1800cm−1) are also detected, indicating that the as-prepared CDs were surrounded by –COOH and –OH groups. These surface groups enable the as-prepared CDs to exhibit good water-solubility and be easily functionalized.

To understand the response rate of the fluorescence signal of CDs to Cu2+, the time-dependent fluorescence changes upon the addition of 10μM Cu2+ were evaluated. As shown in Fig. S1 (ESI†), a substantial fluorescence quenching of CDs was observed within 0.5min of contact time. This rapid response is highly desirable for ion detection. This suggests that Cu2+ initially quenches the fluorescence of CDs by binding to CDs ( Scheme 1), which affects the surface states of CDs and results in the charge transfer from CDs to Cu2+. Following this, the fluorescence quenching became slow until reaching a steady state at 15min. A further increase of time did not lead to any further detectable quenching of the fluorescence of CDs. Therefore, 15min was selected as the optimum reaction time for Cu2+ detection to achieve low detection limit.

Fluorescence titration experiments were performed to investigate the sensitivity of the CDs toward Cu2+. Upon the addition of various concentrations of Cu2+ to CDs with a fixed concentration, the fluorescence intensity changes were investigated upon the excitation at 320nm. As shown in Fig. 2a, the as-prepared CDs initially exhibited a characteristic fluorescence emission band at 460nm. Upon the addition of Cu2+, significant fluorescence quenching can be observed, with the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreasing gradually with the increasing Cu2+ concentration due to the binding of Cu2+ to the surface of CDs. Fig. 2b shows the relationship between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of Cu2+, where the fluorescence intensity can be seen to decrease exponentially with increasing concentration of Cu2+. The relationship is described by the Stern–Volmer equation: F0/F1=1+K sv[Q], where F0 and F1 are the fluorescence intensities of CDs in the absence and presence of Cu2+, respectively, [Q] is the concentration of Cu2+, and K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant. The calibration curve in Fig. 2b (inset) shows a linear relationship (R 2=0.997) of (F1/F0)−1 versus the concentration of Cu2+ over the range from 0 to 10μM. The detection limit (3σ) for Cu2+ was calculated to be 2.3×10−8 M, which is comparable to other reported values using alternative methods (Li et al., 2012b; Tang and Cai, 2012). The results demonstrate that CDs can serve as probes for the detection of Cu2+. To evaluate the repeatability, error bars were also calculated. The corresponding fluorescence quenching and relative standard deviations (RSD) are shown in Table S1. According to the data, over 68% fluorescence intensity of CDs was quenched when the final concentration of Cu2+ was 10μM.

In addition to the sensitivity requirement, high specificity is crucial for the detection. To evaluate the specificity of CDs, we measured the fluorescence changes in the presence of various metal ions including Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+ under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3, some metal ions (Ag+, K+, Na+, Ba2+, and Zn2+) only caused negligible fluorescence change (the change is less than 8%), while some metal ions (Co2+, Fe2+, Hg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, and Fe3+) induced a little fluorescence quenching. Only Cu2+ can lead to the significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity (overall change >68%). The corresponding fluorescence change and RSD values are given in Table S2. The results show that the as-prepared CDs are highly selective toward Cu2+ over the other metal ions.

It is reported that l-Cys could react with Cu2+ due to the existence of thiol group (Li et al., 2013a), therefore, the CDs-Cu2+ system can be explored to probe thiol-containing amino acids based on the competition mechanism between thiol-containing amino acids, Cu2+, and CDs (Li et al., 2012a). Herein, l-Cys was selected as typical thiol-containing amino acid due to its importance in biological processes (Niu et al., 2012; Das et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2009). The data at 0min in Fig. S2 (ESI†) is corresponding to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-Cu2+. Upon the addition of l-Cys to the solution of CDs-Cu2+, the quenched fluorescence of the CDs was recovered immediately within 1min. After 1min, the fluorescence signal stablized, indicating a fast recovery of the system (Fig. S2 and Table S3).

Fig. 4a shows the effect of the amount of l-Cys on the fluorescence recovery of the system. The fluorescence response of the system gradually increases with the increase of l-Cys concentration. The relationship between the fluorescence intensity changes of CDs-Cu2+ and the concentration of l-Cys is shown in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence enhancement can also be described by the Stern–Volmer equation: F2/F1=1+K sv1[Q1], however unlike the previous case for sensing Cu2+, here F1 and F2 are the fluorescence intensities of CDs-Cu2+ in the absence and presence of l-Cys, respectively, [Q1] is the concentration of l-Cys, and K sv1 is the Stern–Volmer constant. The enhancement of fluorescence is proportional to the concentration of l-Cys in the range from 0 to 30μM (R 2=0.999), and the detection limit is 3.4×10−10 M. This sensitivity is higher than that of other reported methods (Li et al., 2013b; Huang et al., 2012). The good repeatability of the sensing system can be observed from the calculated error bars, as shown in Table S4.

To further evaluate the selectivity of l-Cys, the effects of other amino acids (glycine and l-glutamic acid) and GSH on the system were investigated. In addition, other thiol-containing species, such as mercaptoacetic acid and mercaptoethylamine, were also investigated. It can be clearly seen from Fig. 5 and Fig. S3 that only the addition of thiol-containing species can result in the fluorescence enhancement. Thiol-containing species can be bound to Cu2+ to form the Cu2+–S bond (Scheme 1). Thus, Cu2+ was removed from the surface of CDs via competitive adsorption interaction, which makes the fluorescence increase due to avoidance of fluorescence quenching from Cu2+ species (Li et al., 2012a). Most importantly, when adding other amino acids or thiol-containing species, the fluorescence signal of the system was lower than that of the system in the presence of l-Cys (Fig. 5 and Fig. S3). The corresponding fluorescence change and RSD are given in Table S5. The results indicate that the developed sensing system exhibits excellent selectivity of l-Cys.

In conclusion, CDs have been used for “off–on” detection of Cu2+ and l-Cys with high sensitivity and selectivity. Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of CDs through the charge transfer process. Upon the addition of l-Cys into CDs-Cu2+, the fluorescence can be recovered. The likely mechanism relies upon l-Cys being able to effectively shelter the quenching due to the binding of Cu2+ and its thiol group, which removes Cu2+ from the surface of CDs. Detection limits of 2.3×10−8 M for Cu2+ and 3.4×10−10 M for l-Cys were achieved. The strategy presented is facile, rapid, low cost, and environment-friendly, therefore it can be employed to detect Cu2+ and l-Cys in real samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21236003, 21206042, 20925621, 20976054 and 21176083), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (IRT0825), the Shanghai Leading Academic Discipline Project (Project no.: B502), China Scholarship Council, and the Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation (CSIRO) Australia Function Interfaces Research Group for the financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2013.07.042.
